name: Antigua & Barbuda
tagline: 365 Beaches for Every Day of the Year
overview:
Antigua claims 365 beaches — one for every day of the year — and while the number may be generous, the quality is not in question. The island has some of the finest beaches in the Eastern Caribbean, a sailing tradition of international renown and a colonial history that left behind the remarkable Nelson's Dockyard, the only remaining Georgian dockyard in the world still in use.

Antigua's coastline is extraordinary — deeply indented with natural harbors, bays and coves that give it the highest ratio of coastline to land area in the Caribbean. English Harbour, the historic naval base where Horatio Nelson once served, is now a UNESCO World Heritage site and the center of one of the Caribbean's most prestigious sailing communities. The Antigua Sailing Week regatta each April draws competitors from across the world.

Barbuda, the sister island 40 kilometers to the north, offers something completely different — a flat coral island with a pink sand beach 17 miles long, a frigate bird sanctuary of extraordinary size and a population of around 1,800 people living at a pace that the modern world has largely passed by. Barbuda was devastated by Hurricane Irma in 2017 and is slowly rebuilding.

topExperiences.name: Nelson's Dockyard
topExperiences.desc: A UNESCO World Heritage Georgian naval dockyard where Horatio Nelson served — beautifully restored and still functioning as a marina, surrounded by 18th-century stone buildings.

topExperiences.name: Half Moon Bay
topExperiences.desc: A perfect horseshoe of pink and white sand on Antigua's Atlantic coast — protected, beautiful and consistently cited among the Caribbean's finest beaches.

topExperiences.name: Barbuda Frigate Bird Sanctuary
topExperiences.desc: The largest frigate bird colony in the western hemisphere — thousands of birds nesting in the mangroves of Codrington Lagoon, accessible by boat from Barbuda.

topExperiences.name: Antigua Sailing Week
topExperiences.desc: One of the world's great sailing regattas held each April — hundreds of boats racing through extraordinary Caribbean waters with parties that match the competition.

topExperiences.name: Shirley Heights
topExperiences.desc: A former British military lookout with panoramic views over English Harbour — the famous Sunday sunset BBQ and steel pan party is one of the Caribbean's great weekly events.

topExperiences.name: Cades Reef
topExperiences.desc: Antigua's finest snorkeling and diving — a protected reef on the southwest coast with extraordinary coral formations and abundant marine life.

beaches.name: Half Moon Bay
beaches.desc: A protected horseshoe bay of pink-tinged sand and calm clear water — one of the most beautiful natural beaches in the Eastern Caribbean.

beaches.name: Dickenson Bay
beaches.desc: The most developed beach on Antigua — calm, clear and lined with hotels, beach bars and water sports operators.

beaches.name: Pink Beach Barbuda
beaches.desc: 17 miles of pink sand beach on Barbuda — completely undeveloped, accessible only by plane or ferry and one of the most extraordinary beach experiences in the Caribbean.

beaches.name: Darkwood Beach
beaches.desc: A long stretch of white sand on Antigua's west coast — calm water, minimal development and one of the island's most consistently beautiful beaches.

food.name: Fungi & Pepperpot
food.desc: Antigua's national dish — cornmeal fungi (similar to polenta) served with a rich stew of vegetables and salted meat. Simple, deeply satisfying and entirely local.

food.name: Ducana
food.desc: Sweet potato and coconut dumplings wrapped in banana leaves and steamed — the traditional Antiguan side dish that appears at every celebration.

food.name: Wadadli Beer
food.desc: Antigua's local lager, named for the island's original Arawak name — light, crisp and the unofficial official drink of the island.

food.name: Fresh Lobster
food.desc: Spiny Caribbean lobster caught daily around Antigua's reefs — grilled simply with butter and lime at the harbor restaurants of English Harbour.

culture:
Antigua's culture reflects its history as a British sugar colony that achieved independence in 1981 — cricket is the national passion, the steel pan is the national instrument and the relationship with the sea defines everything from the economy to the social calendar. The Antigua Sailing Week and the Cricket World Cup matches played at the Sir Vivian Richards Stadium are the two events that most fully express Antiguan identity.

The island's African heritage, brought through slavery, lives in the music, food and spiritual traditions that persist beneath the British institutional surface. The annual Carnival in late July and early August is the Caribbean expression of that heritage — weeks of calypso, steel pan and costumed parade that represent the fullest expression of Antiguan joy.
bestTime: December through April is the dry season — ideal weather with warm temperatures and minimal rain. April brings Sailing Week. July and August bring Carnival. Hurricane season June through November with Irma in 2017 serving as a devastating reminder of the risk.

practical.currency: Eastern Caribbean Dollar (XCD) · USD widely accepted
practical.howToGet: Direct flights from US, UK and Canada to V.C. Bird International Airport (ANU). Ferry to Barbuda (90 minutes).
